segments.caption: The video opens with a red-tinted title card displaying 'FRONTLINE' over a scene of a Brink’s armored truck outside a Silicon Valley Bank, setting a tone of financial urgency. The scene transitions to black-and-white footage of a crowd gathering outside the bank, with a police officer gesturing to control the crowd, while a camera crew films the event. The 'svb>' logo appears prominently, followed by a shot of the bank’s main lobby, emphasizing the institution’s identity. The narrative shifts to a formal setting with President Joe Biden speaking at a podium flanked by American flags and the presidential seal, suggesting a governmental response to the crisis. The video then cuts to the New York Stock Exchange, showcasing its grand neoclassical architecture and bustling trading floor, symbolizing broader financial markets. A series of interviews with men in suits—some with graying hair and mustaches, others with glasses—appear, likely financial experts or officials, speaking in professional settings. The sequence includes a brief glimpse of a supermarket aisle, possibly illustrating economic impacts on daily life, before returning to a grand, empty boardroom with a long table and ornate decor, hinting at corporate decision-making. The segment concludes with a news broadcast graphic from CNN, showing the Federal Reserve building and a headline about Powell warning of 'some pain' ahead as the Fed fights inflation, tying the bank crisis to national economic policy.

The video returns to the balcony scene, where the men continue their conversation. The final segment features a close-up of Raghuram Rajan, former head of the Indian Central Bank, speaking in an indoor setting, with a serious and reflective expression.

segments.caption: The video opens with a black-and-white shot of Donald Trump seated at a table, gesturing emphatically during a meeting, flanked by men in suits. The scene transitions to color, showing Trump speaking at a podium in the Oval Office, with a CNBC news banner reading 'TRUMP: THE FED HAS 'GONE CRAZY'' and 'WHITE HOUSE DOWNPLAYS WEDNESDAY'S MARKET PLUNGE'. He then appears outdoors in Morristown, New Jersey, in front of Marine One, with a CNN banner stating 'TRUMP ON TRADE WAR: CHINA HAS RIPPED US OFF'. The video cuts to a series of Trump’s tweets criticizing the Federal Reserve, with text highlighting his claims that 'The Fed is like a powerful golfer who can’t putt!' and that 'They don’t have a clue!'. The scene shifts to a black-and-white close-up of Jerome Powell, the Fed Chair, looking pensive and adjusting his watch. Next, a female news anchor in an orange blouse appears on a PBS News Hour set, with a graphic of the Federal Reserve Building and the words 'RATE CUT' behind her. The video then features Dion Rabouin from The Wall Street Journal, speaking in a warmly lit indoor setting with a brick wall background, followed by a black-and-white close-up of Jerome Powell with a stern expression, emphasizing the tension between the President and the Fed.

segments.caption: The video opens with a static shot of the 'BOARD OF GOVERNORS' entrance, flanked by classical columns and abstract art, establishing a formal, institutional atmosphere. The scene transitions to a CNN news broadcast, where anchor Julia Chatterley reports live from New York on the Federal Reserve’s commitment to supporting the U.S. economy amid the coronavirus pandemic, with on-screen graphics displaying global and U.S. case and death totals. The broadcast cuts to Dion Rabouin, identified as a former Axios journalist, speaking in a softly lit indoor setting with a blurred background, wearing a light blue shirt and patterned tie. The video then shifts to exterior shots of the Federal Reserve Building, highlighting its grand architecture, including a detailed stone eagle sculpture and engraved 'FEDERAL RESERVE' lettering under an overcast sky. Next, Chair Jerome Powell appears in a Zoom call, seated before a bookshelf filled with Federal Reserve Bulletins, wearing a dark suit and purple tie, with the 'ZOOM' logo visible. The scene returns to the Federal Reserve Building’s eagle sculpture, then cuts to Lev Menand, an economist formerly at the Fed Reserve Bank of NY (2016–17), speaking in a modern room with exposed brick walls and large windows, wearing a dark blazer and white shirt, with the 'FRONTLINE' logo in the lower third. The sequence ends with a brief shot of another man in a dark shirt, listening intently, in a similar modern indoor setting.

segments.caption: The video opens with an exterior shot of the Federal Reserve Building, its classical white stone facade and prominent columns visible under a clear blue sky, with bare tree branches partially obscuring the view. The camera then cuts to a close-up of Mohamed A. El-Erian, an older man with gray hair and a mustache, wearing a dark suit and blue tie, speaking with a serious and engaged expression in a professional indoor setting with a blurred background. The scene transitions back to the Federal Reserve Building, now showing the engraved 'FEDERAL RESERVE' lettering and a sculpted eagle atop the structure. The video then shifts to a panoramic view of Washington D.C., beginning with a distant shot of the U.S. Capitol Building’s dome rising above surrounding buildings, gradually zooming out to reveal the full Capitol and the Washington Monument in the hazy distance, with the cityscape stretching out under a soft, diffused light. The sequence ends with a black screen, followed by a text overlay reading 'FRONTLINE is made possible by PBS Viewers Like You,' indicating the documentary's funding source and production affiliation.

segments.caption: The video segment consists entirely of a series of static, dark-themed end credits for the FRONTLINE documentary 'Age of Easy Money.' The background is consistently a blurred, abstract digital interface with faint blue light streaks and indistinct screen-like shapes, creating a technological and somber atmosphere. White text appears sequentially, listing production credits including directors, producers, editors, sound, music, and other crew members. The FRONTLINE logo is prominently displayed in red in the top left corner throughout. On-screen text includes acknowledgments to PBS viewers, funding foundations such as the MacArthur Foundation, Park Foundation, Heising-Simons Foundation, and FRONTLINE Journalism Fund, as well as specific support for the episode from 'Chasing the Dream' and 'the Hollyhock foundation.' The credits also list archival sources like CNBC, Fox News, Getty Images, and the National Bureau of Economic Research. Towards the end, a small inset video appears in the bottom right corner showing a black-and-white image of a crowd of unemployed individuals, one holding a sign that reads 'LAID OFF TODAY 9:30 HIRE ME,' with the text 'Age of Easy Money' and 'Available on prime video' below it. The overall tone is formal, informational, and reflective of the documentary’s serious subject matter.

segments.caption: The video segment begins with a dark, digital-themed background featuring blurred, abstract data streams and faint light flares, creating a high-tech, investigative atmosphere. The red 'FRONTLINE' logo appears in the top left corner, followed by white text identifying Raney Aronson-Rath as Executive Producer & Editor-in-Chief. The text then transitions to 'A FRONTLINE production with Left/Right Docs,' with a red lens flare sweeping across the screen, adding visual dynamism. Next, copyright information is displayed: '© 2023 WGBH Educational Foundation ALL RIGHTS RESERVED' and 'FRONTLINE is a production of GBH, which is solely responsible for its content,' reinforcing the documentary’s official and authoritative nature. The screen fades to black, and a stylized, glowing purple 'GBH' logo emerges from the darkness, pulsing slightly before settling into a solid, three-dimensional form, signaling the end of the credits and the production’s branding.
